item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ahhh this is fucked up, good job! 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The downsides are massive, but it's rare for anything, even art, to acknowledge the aspect of it that feels familiar and in a sense comforting even in its horror. Even after getting far enough away from the original circumstances to have the emotional energy necessary to do so, ime there's a big struggle internally to get to the point where the anxiety and stress of contemplating facing life without that emotional armor seems less daunting than the anxiety and stress of leaving it in place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, PTSD is something like an overload. Or, maybe better explained, a short-circuit. It isn't natural, and it isn't more real. It isn't even heightened senses. Fear causes heightened senses.

PTSD kind of freezes a part of the brain on the exact events that caused the trauma. The patient's constant anxiety isn't based on their current circumstances and the "sensation of still being there" actually gets in the way of adaptation to new circumstances. Includes delusions and hallucinations of some degree, and even patients that fully acknowledge it isn't real have a hard time functioning under such level of anxiety, or even directing such anxiety in a new way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My own bias aside, the advice I can give you is to keep in mind that you not saying it doesn't mean that it isn't there. The article shouldn't have so much things thrown in if they are not relevant to the story being told here, but your headcanon for the object can still include those bits.

And then, you can later add them through other works, expanding on your own creation. You can keep record of what you want to be in the skip, and use those as seeds and guides for what you'll do next.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is how I conceptualized it as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's an interesting way of thinking about it. My take would be that PTSD is when the system that makes you averse to things that have scared you before gets overstimulated to the point that it isn't able to work productively anymore, sort of like diabetes or allergies.

The Foundation definitely suspects the Wild God exists, but as far as they can tell he's only been seen in hallucinations and dreams, so they don't really know how to investigate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your criticism is fair and well-taken. I suppose I could say, in the way of explanation, that the intersection of all kinds of fantastical forces, like psionics, memetics, magic, reality bending, deities, etc. is one of the things I find really interesting about the Foundationverse, so those kind of aspects are interesting. But yes, that's a very fair criticism. I consider SCP-3503's somewhat clumsy narrative structure to be the work's biggest flaw, and this is a part of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"But one time, in one "slide," he didn't really do anything to me. He looked at me for like half a second, then he turned around and walked off.

"Somehow that was worse than getting eaten."

thiiiiiisssss +1
+1

The most realistic part of it to me is that the PTSD feels MORE natural than looking at the world any other way. Hell that part (ime) isn't even anomalous. Personal hypothesis on it: we evolved the ability to get PTSD because having a heightened danger response to things that had scared the crap out of us did, at some point, help our ancestors stay alive.

There is something in the soul that craves a simpler time.

Edit: Seems to me it would have occurred to the Foundation that the "wild god" is an independent entity of some kind. At least as far as hypothesizing and making a note that research is ongoing. And I agree the psionic section could be streamlined a bit, and some of the procedures (Crafty, Blarney class maybe) could have carried the same narrative weight with less detail. But overall, I enjoyed it a lot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't upvote here, but just because it really, REALLY over-explains itself. I think the manner you dealt with PTSD was reasonable, and I don't think it tries to explains mass shootings, but just describes one fictional mass shooter. I loved the idea of an anomaly that "rides" a non-anomalous mental condition, it is very interesting and respectful with the fact that the main condition is real and serious.

But seriously, this skip can be hugely trimmed. A lot of it is completely irrelevant to your article, just adding layers and layers of detail to what was already explained. It gets worse when you go to lengths in applying fantastic explanations like psionics and super-memetics just to do with more words what should be just a brief description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't read that as Sarkicism. As Yaldabaoth, sure, but not as Sarkicism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one for the exact same reason you wrote it: this is exactly how a god of nature would be, and the infected worshipping it because of how brutal and uncaring it is makes for interesting characterization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really glad you enjoyed it. You and Daisybellus's comments are really valuable to me, because one of my big worries while writing this was that I might be inadvertantly treating the issue of PTSD in a disrespectful way. I don't have any experiences with PTSD myself, so seeing it well received by people who do is very encouraging.

In regards to your comments on Sarkicism, I largely agree. I actually didn't add the Sarkic tag, nor did the tagging team, another normal user did. I didn't choose to include it because the article largely deals with the conflict between Yaldabaoth and Mekhane, which isn't important to Sarkicism (And also because, in my headcanon, 610 actually isn't Sarkic, it's a direct product of Yaldabaoth himself.). That said, I didn't contest someone else adding it, thinking "Hey, maybe just having Yaldabaoth is enough for the Sarkic tag." And of course more tags means more potential readers, which a writer always wants, so I sort of "gave into temptation." I'll probably remove it now.

Your idea is interesting though. It could even be as simple as "Foundation archaeologist finds a jar made by Proto-Sarkics 1500 years ago that depicts a satyr-like creature, connection to SCP-3503 is speculated." I might add something like that in, and if I do, the Sarkic tag can come back as well.
Fun-fact your idea reminded me of, I considered including a brief passage where The Foundation asked some of it's contacts in the Serpent's Hand to ask actual Satyrs if they knew anything about 3503. I decided not to include it to avoid over-complicating the narrative, and because I want to use a similar "zinger" in another article I'm going to write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As somebody who has a few close friends with PTSD (both classic and complex), and who has seen the effect it has on them up close, the thought of an contagious form of it with vectors actively seeking to spread it is, to me, extremely disconcerting. It's also why I really liked this article. +1!

That aside, from the article itself, I can't really see how this is tied to Sarkicisim. I get the reference to -610 and your headcanon, but the first is (unfortunately, IMO) not regarded as canonically Sarkic, while the second usually doesn't merit tags. Don't get me wrong, I don't mind more Sarkic material, I just believe it needs something more substantial to go be tagged as "sarkic". Maybe some local (Pashto?) oral legend or other narrative about Sarkic activity in the area long ago, steeped in myth yet somehow tied to the keloid satyr. Just a thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting read involving even more interesting god characters a solid +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you liked it! I appreciate the suggestions for improving those sentences, I'll implement them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, the execution isnt well enough to overcome my inherent discomfort regarding the topic. Narrow downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I'm glad you liked it.

I'll have to speak in my own defense here, I'm not trying to explain mass shootings in the article. The great majority of mass shootings are done with the intent of killing a lot of people, but SCP-3503 infectees who do mass shootings want to kill as few people as possible, because dead people can't get PTSD. I can't think of a real life mass shooting that could be attributed to SCP-3503 in the foundation universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love goat god of the flesh, but I hate SCP explains mass shootings. This is a narrow +1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty interesting idea! As someone with PTSD, it was cool to read about something anomalous pertaining to it. I also have ADHD, though, so I wasn't really able to absorb 100% of it.

Either way, there's a few grammatical issues like run-on sentences.

  • "In 2011, he attacked the life insurance company where he worked, there were no fatalities but 34 serious injuries." (could use a semicolon after "worked" and a comma after "fatalities")
  • "Most begin exhibiting uncharacteristically abusive behavior towards family and close friends. Some attempt to commit acts of mass violence, such as a mass shooting or public bombing, generally planned in a manner which minimizes fatalities but maximizes injuries and induced panic. " Could be rewritten, maybe like this: "Most individuals at this stage begin exhibiting uncharacteristically abusive behavior towards people in their lives while some go as far as to commit acts of mass violence (e.g. a mass shooting, a public bombing). These acts of mass or individual terrorism focus not on fatalities, but instead on injuries; panic; and overall stress."

I would do more constructive stuff, but it's super late. Good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wanted to second that "pecker" kind of threw me out of the story for a brief moment.

item.title: Re: AUTHOR POST
item.content:encoded: Your point gets across either way! Glad you enjoyed.

item.title: Re: AUTHOR POST
item.content:encoded:
Reads like a smooth gin and tonic.

Not like I've ever had one, though. +1

(Edit: forgot I have to click on "New Post", sorry)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The point about the explanatory note is a very fair criticism. I considered this while writing, but I couldn't come up with a better way to do it.

I guess "pecker" is a holdover from when Baker was a Vietnam war vet. I kept it because I thought it had a bit better cadence than dick, but dick does sound more natural. I'll give it a second look tomorrow. Thanks for your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little rough around the edges (mainly due to the over-reliance of long explanatory note at the end syndrome) but overall not bad, not bad at all. Takes a new look at an aspect of Sarkicism that hasn’t been explored much previously and the imagery is pretty decent too. +1

Side Note: I’d reccomend adjusting the diction a little. For example, no one really says pecker in America these days, especially a soldier in this context.

item.title: AUTHOR POST
item.content:encoded:
If you're interested in my other work, here is my author page

I think it was 2013 when I discovered the SCP Foundation, and I was immediately entranced. After years of lurking, this is my first attempt at writing for the site. I would love to hear your thoughts.

Big thanks to Croquembouche and Peter Dawley for giving me their feedback in chat.

The seed of the idea was when I was thinking about Gods of nature and how I didn't find them plausible. They were often too human, too sensible and kind to be manifestations of uncontrolled nature. Then I realized that there was one god of nature I'd read about who I thought fit the bill fantastically, even though he isn't called a "god of nature." That god is Yaldabaoth.

Now I'm a firm believer that there is no canon, and you can interpret 3503 in any manner you wish, or no manner. But, here's what the humble author feels about it….

+ Possible Spoilers
- collapse

The wild god Baker encountered is, in some way, Pan. It is also, in some way, a manifestation of Yaldabaoth. How exactly this works out is up to your interpretation, dear reader.

SCP-3503, more broadly, is a manifestation of Yaldabaoth's hatred of Mekhane and her gift to the intelligent peoples of the universe.

This is why the Church of the Broken God kidnapped Baker. His experience is of theological interest to the church. He's off being dissected/experimented on right about now.

I hope y'all enjoy.
